narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you've brought to this journey. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 5, which is slightly below the typical student's 5.71, a Year 3 decile of 3—quite a bit lower than the average 5.53—middle family income, an LSAT score of 25 compared to the usual 36.7, an undergraduate GPA of 2.4 against an average of 3.21, and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from many past students, estimated a 71% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize those with stronger signals of bar passage. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as it fell well short of what typically leads to success. Your Year 3 decile ranking played a significant secondary role in the same direction, signaling a dip in later academic performance relative to peers. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile actually worked in your favor, suggesting stronger early momentum than the model expected. Your undergraduate GPA also nudged the prediction toward failure, though less strongly, while your full-time status and family income had minimal sway. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your key metrics like LSAT, later deciles, and GPA are noticeably lower, which tipped the scales here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying once you've addressed these areas—many students in similar situations have succeeded with targeted improvements. We're rooting for you to turn this around and join the legal profession.
